summary:
The Pagan Kingdom (849–1297 AD) was the first Burmese empire to unify the region of modern Myanmar, established by King Anawrahta (reigned 1044–1077) who conquered the Mon kingdom of Thaton in 1057 and brought its Theravada Buddhist monks, scholars, and sacred texts to his capital of Pagan — an act of cultural appropriation that transformed Burma into the preeminent center of Theravada Buddhism in mainland Southeast Asia and initiated an extraordinary period of temple construction along the Irrawaddy River.

The Pagan plain witnessed one of the greatest concentrations of religious building in world history: over a period of roughly two centuries, approximately 10,000 temples, pagodas, and monasteries were constructed in a relatively small area, of which over 2,000 survive. The temple-building program was driven by the Burmese concept of merit-accumulation through religious construction — kings, nobles, and wealthy merchants all funded religious buildings to accumulate spiritual merit — creating a landscape of extraordinary architectural richness that is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The artistic tradition of Pagan Buddhism — its murals, sculptures, and architectural forms — established the aesthetic vocabulary of Burmese Buddhism that persists to the present day. The kingdom was severely weakened by the [[event:91|Mongol]] invasions of 1277 and 1287 (Kublai Khan's armies occupied Pagan briefly) and by the political consequences of the enormous resources devoted to temple-building, which starved the military and agricultural infrastructure. The kingdom collapsed in 1297, fragmenting into smaller states until the reunification of Burma under the Toungoo Dynasty in the 16th century.
